content_en:
The muamalat (commercial transactions) section of Al-Mughni represents one of the most important contributions of classical Hanbali jurisprudence to Islamic law. Ibn Qudamah covers contracts of sale, partnership, agency, hire, loan, pledge, and guarantee with the same rigor he applies to worship, reflecting the Islamic understanding that commercial life is equally subject to divine guidance.

The foundational principle of Islamic commercial law is the permissibility of trade and the prohibition of riba (interest/usury). The Quran states directly: "Allah has permitted trade and forbidden riba" (2:275). All commercial transactions must conform to this principle: they must involve genuine exchange of value, clear knowledge of subject matter and price, willing consent of both parties, and freedom from excessive uncertainty (gharar) and prohibited elements.

The contract of sale (bay') in the Hanbali school requires: an offer (ijab) and acceptance (qabul), the capacity of both parties to contract, that the subject matter and price be known and determinate, that the goods exist and be deliverable, and that both parties have the right to dispose of what they are selling. Ibn Qudamah discusses the extensive categories of prohibited sales: selling what one does not own or possess, selling goods before taking possession (with exceptions for currency exchange), sales involving unknown quantities (selling fish in the sea, birds in the sky), and sales involving a prohibited item (alcohol, pork).

The prohibition of riba is treated with great care. Riba has two main forms: riba al-fadl (excess in exchange of like goods of the same genus — the Prophet forbade exchanging gold for more gold, wheat for more wheat, etc., except in equal amounts and hand to hand, Bukhari) and riba al-nasi'ah (delay in the exchange of goods belonging to the ribawi categories). Ibn Qudamah discusses the six ribawi items established by the hadith (gold, silver, wheat, barley, dates, salt) and the extension of these categories to other similar goods by analogy.

Partnerships (sharikah) receive detailed treatment: Hanbali law recognizes the partnership of capital (sharikah al-inan), the partnership of bodies (mufawadah, in which partners have equal shares and unlimited authority), the partnership of labor (sharikah al-abdan, in which craftsmen pool their skills), and the partnership of reputation (sharikah al-wujuh, in which the partners' creditworthiness is the asset). The rules of profit and loss sharing, the liability of partners, and the conditions for valid partnership are addressed.

The mudarabah contract — in which one party provides capital and another provides labor, with profits shared by agreement and losses borne entirely by the capital provider — is recognized as one of the foundational instruments of Islamic commercial finance. Ibn Qudamah establishes its conditions: that the capital be known, that the profit share be a fraction (not a fixed sum), and that the worker-manager (mudarib) not guarantee the capital.

Ibn Qudamah also addresses the rules of salam (advance purchase with deferred delivery) — a contract the Prophet explicitly permitted as an exception to the general prohibition of selling what one does not yet possess — as well as istisna' (commissioning manufactured goods), hire (ijarah), pledge (rahn), and guarantee (kafalah).

The breadth of Al-Mughni's muamalat section reflects the classical Islamic understanding: commerce is worship when conducted according to Allah's law, and the scholar's duty is to guide believers through the complexities of economic life with the same care given to ritual practice.
content_id:
Kitab Al-Mughni memberikan perhatian sangat besar pada hukum muamalat (transaksi komersial), mengakui bahwa kehidupan ekonomi umat Islam harus diatur oleh prinsip-prinsip syariat yang jelas. Ibn Qudamah membahas berbagai jenis transaksi dengan kedalaman analisis yang menunjukkan pemahaman mendalam beliau tentang realitas ekonomi dan dalil-dalil syariat.

Topik-topik yang dibahas mencakup: jual beli dan syarat-syaratnya, riba dan jenis-jenisnya, salam (jual beli pesanan), ijarah (sewa-menyewa), mudharabah (kemitraan modal), musyarakah (kerjasama usaha), qardh (pinjaman), rahn (gadai), wakalah (perwakilan), syuf'ah (hak preemptif), waqaf, hibah, wasiat, hukum-hukum kepailitan, serta berbagai transaksi modern yang dapat diqiyaskan dengan transaksi-transaksi klasik dalam fikih Hanbali.
